# Preface {.unnumbered}

This guide grew out of frustration — not with my students, but with a pattern I kept seeing. Talented PhD students and postdocs would produce manuscript drafts 
that were theoretically exhaustive but argumentatively weak. Every relevant theory got a paragraph. Every related concept got a mention. The papers were comprehensive and unreadable.

The problem wasn't knowledge. My students knew the literature well — sometimes too well. The problem was a set of unwritten rules nobody had told them about: how to decide which theories are load-bearing for your argument and which are not, how to write for a reader who needs to be convinced rather than impressed, and how to survive a review process that sometimes incentivizes the wrong things.

This problem has gotten worse. The arrival of AI writing tools has accelerated a tendency that was already there. Ask an AI assistant to improve your introduction 
and it will suggest three more theories you haven't mentioned. The writing comes back polished and bloated, and it sounds authoritative enough that the underlying 
problem — a missing argument — becomes harder to see.

This guide is my attempt to write the unwritten rules down. It is not a comprehensive textbook on scientific writing — there are excellent books for that. 
It is a practical field guide for researchers who already know their science and are struggling with turning it into a paper that works. The final chapter addresses AI writing tools directly — not to discourage their use, but to help you use them as a sharp tool rather than a crutch.

The examples are drawn from cognitive psychology and event cognition, but the principles apply broadly. The specific problem this guide addresses — too many theories, too little argument — is most acute in theory-rich fields, where phenomena attract multiple competing accounts and reviewing culture reinforces citation of all of them. In fields with tighter theoretical consensus, or where data more reliably rule out competing accounts — physics being the obvious extreme — the pressure is lower. Psychology is a theory-rich field: phenomena attract multiple accounts, and few are falsified cleanly enough to clear the landscape. But the tools here — finding an anchor, applying the filters, keeping the Discussion honest — are useful wherever you write a paper that makes a theoretical claim. If you are working in neuroscience, clinical psychology, or an adjacent field, the norms around theoretical framing may differ from what is described here; treat the specific advice as a starting point rather than a prescription.

If you are a PhD student preparing your first manuscript, a postdoc revising a rejected paper, or a supervisor looking for something to hand your students — this is for you.
